caveats: a: OEWS covers all foundries rather than 331524.
a: Sand, investment, and permanent-mold operations have materially different labor mixes.
rho: No representative LMM implementation study was found.
rho: Casting simulation and conventional optimization already address part of the opportunity and must not be relabeled as new AI benefit.
e: Eligibility is not directly measured.
e: The injected n=107 uses a machinery-sector margin bridge despite the industry's unusually high frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio.
s5: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
s5: Age is not an observed sale or succession-completion rate.
q: No observed retention sample was found.
q: Retention differs between short-run engineered castings and higher-volume price-competed programs.
d5: AFS sales growth is nominal, short-term, and covers all metalcasting.
d5: BLS foundry output is broader than aluminum and not a physical constant-quality quantity series.
o: Manufacturing-process substitution is captured principally in d5.
o: Digital ordering and reporting can be self-served without removing the physical operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring engineered business-to-business casting programs priced per part or lot with separate pattern, tooling, engineering, finishing, testing, and metal-surcharge economics under quotes, releases, purchase orders, and supply agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Die casters, captive foundries, establishments further manufacturing castings into their own finished products, primary aluminum producers, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent US aluminum foundries outside die casting, in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band, repeatedly producing customer-designed sand, permanent-mold, semi-permanent-mold, investment, or related castings for external customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all foundries and national casting-machine projections, not wage-weighted task exposure in 331524. Physical molding, coremaking, melting, pouring, shakeout, finishing, inspection, maintenance, and handling were largely excluded, while quoting, methoding support, documentation, scheduling, quality analysis, maintenance knowledge, and administration were partly exposed; broader data may understate engineering work in complex low-volume aluminum castings.
caveats: OEWS covers all foundries rather than 331524.
Sand, investment, and permanent-mold operations have materially different labor mixes.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from independent aluminum foundries by casting process, explicitly documenting current simulation, ERP, inspection, molding, pouring, finishing, and maintenance automation.
a.rationale: Non-die aluminum casting uses labor-intensive physical mold and core preparation plus molten-metal and finishing work. AI can assist information, diagnostic, and coordination tasks, but most labor substitution would require robotics, mechanization, or new casting equipment rather than AI alone.

d5.bridge: BLS projects all-foundry real output declining 0.8% annually through 2034, while AFS projects one-year casting sales growth across all metals and processes. Applying a near-flat five-year path to 331524 can understate aluminum lightweighting, aerospace, and reshoring or overstate demand where automotive architecture, imports, or process substitution reduce conventional castings.
caveats: AFS sales growth is nominal, short-term, and covers all metalcasting.
BLS foundry output is broader than aluminum and not a physical constant-quality quantity series.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US aluminum casting shipments by sand, permanent mold, investment, and end market, reconciled with customer awards, aerospace and vehicle production, import penetration, additive substitution, reshoring, and capacity closures.
d5.rationale: BLS expects foundry real output to decline from $26.9 billion in 2024 to $24.8 billion in 2034, but AFS projected 1.9% casting-sales growth for 2025. Aluminum's lightweight properties and diverse end uses offset some broad foundry weakness, supporting a near-flat central case with a wide range.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly measured.
The injected n=107 uses a machinery-sector margin bridge despite the industry's unusually high frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen n population with external revenue, captive status, casting process, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, equipment condition, environmental liabilities, certifications, tooling ownership, and stand-alone management.
e.rationale: Independent aluminum foundries commonly provide recurring outsourced castings and fit the operating lens. Captive plants, vertically integrated finished-product manufacturers, customer concentration, environmental exposure, outdated equipment, weak management depth, and size-band errors exclude a meaningful minority.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331524 ($1,033,382,086) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331524 ($2,879,262,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $238,093 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331524); class <5: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $776,947 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $152,437 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,128,394 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $221,391 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,970,389 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $386,590 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 83 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,552,361 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,677,973 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,347,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,580,681 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $97,108,733 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,052,733 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 107

caveats: Manufacturing-process substitution is captured principally in d5.
Digital ordering and reporting can be self-served without removing the physical operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly internalizing aluminum casting, additive or fabrication methods eliminating cast parts, or autonomous foundries operating without accountable production, metallurgy, and quality management.
o.rationale: Cast aluminum components require accountable mold and core preparation, metallurgy, melting, pouring, solidification control, finishing, inspection, traceability, and delivery. Software and automated equipment can improve tasks but do not eliminate the responsible foundry operator within five years.

caveats: No observed retention sample was found.
Retention differs between short-run engineered castings and higher-volume price-competed programs.
q.change_evidence: Representative contracts and program margin histories showing aluminum surcharges, open-book provisions, cost-down requirements, qualification and tooling ownership, renewal cadence, and margin persistence after documented productivity gains.
q.rationale: Complex low- and medium-volume castings can retain benefits from reduced scrap, shorter development, better uptime, and reliable delivery because qualification and pattern knowledge create switching costs. Metal pass-through, competitive quotes, customer cost-down demands, and rebids still share recurring gains.

caveats: No representative LMM implementation study was found.
Casting simulation and conventional optimization already address part of the opportunity and must not be relabeled as new AI benefit.
rho.change_evidence: Audited aluminum-foundry AI deployments showing labor hours, quote cycle time, scrap, first-pass yield, lead time, downtime, false defect calls, customer requalification, and required review before and after deployment.
rho.rationale: Quote retrieval, routing drafts, schedule support, defect-pattern analysis, quality documentation, maintenance retrieval, purchasing, and administration are implementable within five years. Small-firm systems, low-volume data, part-specific metallurgy, customer validation, safety, traceability, and human engineering approval limit deployment.

s5.bridge: Census reports ages of responding employer-business owners across all industries, not aluminum-foundry control transfers. A portion of the 51% age-55-plus owner population plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into five-year qualifying transfers after excluding family succession, continued ownership, closure, and nontransferable operations; family-held shops may make the proxy overstate sales, while foundry consolidation may make it understate them.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
Age is not an observed sale or succession-completion rate.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of completed, failed, and withdrawn transactions for US aluminum foundries in the EBITDA band, paired with current owner-age, succession, customer-consent, and environmental interviews.
s5.rationale: Owner aging creates succession pressure, and qualified programs, patterns, know-how, and equipment can support a sale. Environmental diligence, customer approvals, capital needs, and owner-dependent relationships reduce sale completion.

principal_driver: High labor intensity and repeat engineered programs create useful AI support opportunities around quoting, methoding, quality, maintenance, and scheduling.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and part-specific, while broad foundry demand is projected to contract and implementation data are scarce.

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as aluminum foundries producing castings other than die castings and distinguishes establishments that further manufacture castings into finished products.
sources.title: NAICS 331524: Aluminum Foundries (except Die-Casting)

sources.supports: BLS provides employer-survey occupational employment and wage estimates for foundries, used as the broader occupation-mix evidence.
sources.title: Foundries - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: BLS reports 154,600 molding, coremaking, and casting machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 148,800 in 2034, a 3.8% decline.
sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024-2034

sources.supports: EPA states that smaller-emitting aluminum, copper, and other nonferrous foundries are subject to standards regulating beryllium, cadmium, chromium, lead, manganese, nickel, and other toxic emissions.
sources.title: Aluminum, Copper, and Other Nonferrous Foundries: NESHAP for Area Sources

sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older, covering 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: BLS projects foundry employment declining 14.7% from 106,000 in 2024 to 90,400 in 2034 and real output declining from $26.9 billion to $24.8 billion, a 0.8% annual rate.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: The American Foundry Society projected 1.9% casting sales growth for 2025 in its Metalcasting Forecast & Trends release.
sources.title: AFS Projects 1.9 Percent Casting Sales Growth for 2025

title: Aluminum Foundries (except Die-Casting)
